example_sentence: A class is a blueprint for objects.
An object is an instance of a class.
A method is a function defined in a class.
An attribute stores data in an object.
Inheritance allows a class to inherit from another.
An exception occurs when an error happens.

example_sentence: An array stores elements in contiguous memory.

stack.pop()

example_sentence: A queue follows First-In-First-Out order.

q.append(1)
q.popleft()

example_sentence: A linked list stores data with pointers to the next node.
A tree is a hierarchical data structure.
A binary tree has at most two children per node.
A graph consists of vertices and edges.
A hash table provides O(1) average lookup time.
Each element in a linked list is a node.
A pointer references a memory location.
The head is the first node of a linked list.
code_snippet:
head = Node(1)
head.next = Node(2)

example_sentence: The tail is the last node of a linked list.
Push adds an element to the top of the stack.
code_snippet:
stack = []
stack.append(10)
print(stack)

example_sentence: Pop removes the top element from the stack.
code_snippet:
stack = [1, 2, 3]
top = stack.pop()
print(top)

example_sentence: Enqueue adds an element to the back of the queue.
code_snippet:
from collections import deque
q = deque()
q.append(1)

example_sentence: Dequeue removes the front element from the queue.
code_snippet:
from collections import deque
q = deque([1, 2, 3])
q.popleft()

example_sentence: A priority queue serves the highest priority element first.
code_snippet:
import heapq

heapq.heappush(heap, 3)
heapq.heappush(heap, 1)

example_sentence: A heap is a complete binary tree with heap property.
code_snippet:

example_sentence: A BST keeps left < root < right.
Tree traversal visits every node exactly once.
The depth of a node is its distance from the root.
Breadth-first search explores level by level.
code_snippet:
from collections import deque
q = deque([root])
while q:
    node = q.popleft()

example_sentence: An adjacency list represents graph connections.
An adjacency matrix uses a 2D array for graph edges.

An edge connects two vertices in a graph.
A weighted graph assigns cost to each edge.

A dynamic array resizes automatically.

example_sentence: Search finds an element in a data structure.
Binary search halves the search space each step.
Linear search checks every element one by one.
Bubble sort swaps adjacent elements repeatedly.
Quick sort uses a pivot to partition the array.
Merge sort divides and merges sorted halves.
Insertion sort builds sorted array one element at a time.
Selection sort finds the minimum and swaps it.
A greedy algorithm makes locally optimal choices.
code_snippet:
coins = [25, 10, 5, 1]
amount = 30
count = 0
for c in coins:
    count += amount // c
    amount %= c

example_sentence: Dynamic programming solves problems by combining sub-solutions.
Backtracking explores all possible solutions.
Divide and conquer splits problem into subproblems.
Brute force tries every possible solution.
A hash function maps data to a fixed-size value.
Complexity measures time or space requirements.
Time complexity measures how runtime grows with input.
Space complexity measures memory usage.
code_snippet:
# O(n) space
arr = [0] * n

example_sentence: Big O describes the upper bound of complexity.
O(1) means constant time regardless of input size.
O(n) time grows linearly with input size.
code_snippet:
for item in arr:
    print(item)  # O(n)

example_sentence: O(log n) halves the problem each step.
code_snippet:
while lo <= hi:
    mid = (lo + hi) // 2  # O(log n)

example_sentence: An optimal solution uses minimum resources.
Dynamic programming solves subproblems first.
Memoization caches function results.
Tabulation builds solution bottom-up.
Iteration uses loops instead of recursion.
Two pointers traverse from both ends.
Sliding window maintains a range over data.
code_snippet:
def max_sum(arr, k):
    window = sum(arr[:k])
    max_val = window
    for i in range(k, len(arr)):
        window += arr[i] - arr[i-k]
        max_val = max(max_val, window)
    return max_val

example_sentence: DFS explores as deep as possible first.
BFS explores level by level using a queue.
Find the shortest path between two nodes.
Greedy choice picks the best local option.
code_snippet:
# Activity selection
activities.sort(key=lambda x: x[1])
selected = [activities[0]]

example_sentence: Overlapping subproblems share the same sub-solutions.
code_snippet:
# fib(5) needs fib(4) and fib(3)
# fib(4) also needs fib(3) -> overlap

example_sentence: State represents the current situation in DP.
code_snippet:
dp = [[0]*m for _ in range(n)]
dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] + dp[i][j-1]

example_sentence: State transition defines how to move between states.
code_snippet:
# dp[i] = max(dp[i-1], dp[i-2]+nums[i])
dp[i] = max(dp[i-1], dp[i-2]+nums[i])

example_sentence: Base case initializes the DP table.
Edge cases are unusual input conditions.
Corner cases combine multiple edge conditions.
In-place algorithms use O(1) extra space.
code_snippet:
arr.reverse()  # in-place
# vs sorted(arr) which creates new list

example_sentence: A stable sort preserves relative order of equal elements.
code_snippet:

arr.sort(key=lambda x: x[0])
# (1,'a') stays before (1,'c')

example_sentence: A recursive function calls itself.
The base case prevents infinite recursion.
Swap two elements in an array.
code_snippet:
a, b = 1, 2
a, b = b, a
print(a, b)

example_sentence: Partition divides array around a pivot.
The pivot is the reference element in quicksort.
code_snippet:
pivot = arr[len(arr)//2]
left = [x for x in arr if x < pivot]

example_sentence: A repository stores all project files and history.
code_snippet:
git init
git clone https://github.com/user/repo.git

example_sentence: A commit saves changes to the local repository.
code_snippet:
git add .
git commit -m "add feature"

example_sentence: A branch is an independent line of development.
code_snippet:
git branch feature
git checkout feature

example_sentence: Merge combines changes from different branches.
code_snippet:
git checkout main
git merge feature

example_sentence: Clone creates a local copy of a remote repository.
code_snippet: git clone https://github.com/user/repo.git

example_sentence: Push uploads local commits to the remote repository.
code_snippet: git push origin main

example_sentence: Pull downloads changes from the remote repository.
code_snippet: git pull origin main

example_sentence: Fetch downloads changes without merging.
code_snippet: git fetch origin

example_sentence: Stage prepares changes for the next commit.
code_snippet:
git add file.py
git status

example_sentence: The staging area holds changes before committing.
code_snippet:
git add .
git diff --staged

example_sentence: The working directory contains your project files.
code_snippet:
git status
pwd

example_sentence: A remote is a version of the repository hosted elsewhere.
code_snippet:
git remote -v
git remote add origin URL

example_sentence: Origin is the default name for the remote repository.
code_snippet: git push origin main

example_sentence: Main is the default branch name.
code_snippet:
git checkout main
git merge feature

example_sentence: HEAD points to the current commit.
code_snippet:
git log --oneline -5
# HEAD -> main

example_sentence: Diff shows changes between commits or files.
code_snippet:
git diff
git diff --staged

example_sentence: Log shows the commit history.
code_snippet:
git log --oneline
git log --graph

example_sentence: Status shows the current state of the working directory.
code_snippet: git status

example_sentence: Checkout switches to a different branch.
code_snippet:
git checkout feature
git checkout -b new-branch

example_sentence: Stash temporarily saves uncommitted changes.
code_snippet:
git stash
git stash pop

example_sentence: A conflict occurs when merging incompatible changes.
code_snippet:
# After merge conflict:
git diff
# Fix manually, then:
git add .
git commit

example_sentence: Resolve conflicts by editing the conflicted files.
code_snippet:
# Edit file to fix <<<< ==== >>>>
git add resolved_file.py
git commit

example_sentence: Rebase moves commits to a new base branch.
code_snippet:
git checkout feature
git rebase main

example_sentence: Cherry-pick applies a specific commit to another branch.
code_snippet:
git checkout main
git cherry-pick abc123

example_sentence: A tag marks a specific commit as important.
code_snippet:
git tag v1.0
git push origin v1.0

example_sentence: Fork creates a personal copy of someone else's repository.

# On GitHub: click Fork button
git clone https://github.com/YOUR/repo.git

example_sentence: A pull request proposes changes to be merged.
code_snippet:
# On GitHub: click New Pull Request
git push origin feature

example_sentence: An issue tracks bugs or feature requests.
code_snippet:
# On GitHub: click New Issue
# Link in commit: fixes #42

*.pyc

example_sentence: A commit message describes what changed and why.

example_sentence: Each commit is a snapshot of the project state.

def456 snapshot 2

example_sentence: HTTP is the protocol for web communication.

example_sentence: A URL specifies the address of a resource.

url = "https://example.com/api/data"

example_sentence: A request is sent from client to server.

example_sentence: A response is sent from server to client.

example_sentence: 200 means the request was successful.
404 means the resource was not found.
500 means an internal server error occurred.

client = requests.Session()
r = client.get(url)

example_sentence: The server processes requests and returns responses.
An endpoint is a specific API URL path.
A route maps a URL pattern to a handler.

def about(): return "About page"

example_sentence: Middleware processes requests before they reach the handler.

@app.middleware("http")
async def add_header(request, call_next):
    response = await call_next(request)
    return response

example_sentence: A webhook sends real-time notifications to a URL.

while True:
    r = requests.get(url)
    if r.json().get("done"): break
    time.sleep(1)

import websockets

example_sentence: A database stores and organizes data.
code_snippet:
import sqlite3
conn = sqlite3.connect("mydb.db")

example_sentence: A table stores data in rows and columns.
A row represents a single record.
code_snippet:
INSERT INTO users (name) VALUES ('Alice');
SELECT * FROM users;

example_sentence: A column defines a field in a table.
code_snippet:
ALTER TABLE users ADD email TEXT;
SELECT name, email FROM users;

example_sentence: SQL is the standard language for databases.
code_snippet: SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = 1;

example_sentence: A query retrieves data from the database.
code_snippet: SELECT name, email FROM users WHERE age > 18;

example_sentence: SELECT retrieves data from a table.
code_snippet: SELECT id, name FROM users ORDER BY name;

example_sentence: INSERT adds new rows to a table.
code_snippet: INSERT INTO users (name, email) VALUES ('Alice', 'alice@test.com');

example_sentence: UPDATE modifies existing rows.
code_snippet: UPDATE users SET email = 'new@email.com' WHERE id = 1;

example_sentence: DELETE removes rows from a table.
code_snippet: DELETE FROM users WHERE id = 1;

example_sentence: WHERE filters rows based on a condition.
code_snippet: SELECT * FROM users WHERE age >= 18;

example_sentence: JOIN combines rows from multiple tables.
A primary key uniquely identifies each row.
A foreign key links to another table's primary key.
An index speeds up data retrieval.
code_snippet:
CREATE INDEX idx_email ON users(email);
SELECT * FROM users WHERE email = 'test@test.com';

example_sentence: A schema defines the database structure.
ORM maps database tables to Python classes.

from sqlalchemy.orm import DeclarativeBase
class Base(DeclarativeBase): pass

example_sentence: Migration applies schema changes to the database.
code_snippet:
# alembic migration
alembic upgrade head
alembic downgrade -1

example_sentence: A transaction groups operations that must all succeed.
Commit saves all changes in a transaction.
code_snippet: db.commit()

example_sentence: Rollback undoes all changes in a transaction.
ACID ensures reliable database transactions.
Normalization reduces data redundancy.
code_snippet:
# Before: users(id, name, order1, order2)
# After: users(id, name) + orders(id, user_id)

example_sentence: Aggregate functions summarize multiple rows.
code_snippet: SELECT COUNT(*), AVG(age) FROM users GROUP BY city;

example_sentence: GROUP BY groups rows with the same value.
code_snippet: SELECT city, COUNT(*) FROM users GROUP BY city;

example_sentence: ORDER BY sorts the result set.
code_snippet: SELECT * FROM users ORDER BY name ASC;

example_sentence: LIMIT restricts the number of rows returned.
code_snippet: SELECT * FROM users ORDER BY id LIMIT 10;

example_sentence: OFFSET skips a number of rows for pagination.
code_snippet: SELECT * FROM users ORDER BY id LIMIT 10 OFFSET 20;

example_sentence: COUNT returns the number of rows.
code_snippet: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM users WHERE age > 18;

example_sentence: SUM adds up numeric values.
code_snippet: SELECT SUM(amount) FROM orders WHERE user_id = 1;

example_sentence: AVG calculates the mean value.
code_snippet: SELECT AVG(age) FROM users;

example_sentence: MAX returns the highest value.
code_snippet: SELECT MAX(score) FROM results;

example_sentence: MIN returns the lowest value.
code_snippet: SELECT MIN(price) FROM products;

example_sentence: HAVING filters groups after GROUP BY.
code_snippet:
SELECT city, COUNT(*) as cnt
FROM users
GROUP BY city
HAVING cnt > 5;

example_sentence: UNION combines results from multiple SELECT statements.
A subquery is a query inside another query.
code_snippet:
SELECT * FROM users
WHERE id IN (SELECT user_id FROM orders);

example_sentence: A view is a virtual table based on a query.
code_snippet:
CREATE VIEW active_users AS
SELECT * FROM users WHERE status = 'active';

example_sentence: A trigger runs automatically on table events.
A stored procedure is a precompiled SQL routine.
A constraint enforces rules on data.
NULL represents missing or unknown data.
code_snippet: SELECT * FROM users WHERE email IS NULL;

example_sentence: NOT NULL prevents a column from having NULL values.
UNIQUE ensures all values in a column are different.
DEFAULT provides a value when none is specified.
ALTER TABLE modifies an existing table structure.
code_snippet:
ALTER TABLE users ADD phone TEXT;
ALTER TABLE users DROP COLUMN phone;

example_sentence: DROP TABLE permanently deletes a table.
code_snippet: DROP TABLE IF EXISTS temp_data;

example_sentence: TRUNCATE removes all rows but keeps the table structure.
code_snippet: TRUNCATE TABLE logs;

example_sentence: SQLite is a lightweight file-based database.

cursor = conn.cursor()

example_sentence: A cursor executes SQL and fetches results.

cursor.execute("SELECT * FROM users")
rows = cursor.fetchall()

example_sentence: A decorator modifies a function's behavior.
A generator yields values lazily one at a time.

square = lambda x: x ** 2
print(square(5))

nums = [1, 2, 3]
print(sorted(nums, key=lambda x: -x))

example_sentence: List comprehension creates lists concisely.
code_snippet:
squares = [x**2 for x in range(10)]
evens = [x for x in range(20) if x % 2 == 0]
print(squares, evens)

example_sentence: Dictionary comprehension creates dicts concisely.
Enumerate adds a counter to an iterable.
Zip pairs elements from multiple iterables.
code_snippet:
names = ['Alice', 'Bob']
ages = [25, 30]
for name, age in zip(names, ages):
    print(name, age)

example_sentence: Map applies a function to every element.
code_snippet:

squared = list(map(lambda x: x**2, nums))
print(squared)

example_sentence: Filter keeps elements that pass a test.
code_snippet:

evens = list(filter(lambda x: x % 2 == 0, nums))
print(evens)

example_sentence: Reduce applies a function cumulatively to elements.

print(sum_val)

example_sentence: Try-except catches and handles exceptions.

example_sentence: Open reads or writes files.

    return 'done'

result = asyncio.run(fetch_data())

    print(result)

asyncio.run(main())

example_sentence: __init__ initializes a new object.
Self refers to the current instance of a class.
Property creates managed attributes.
Static methods don't need a class instance.
Class methods receive the class as first argument.
Super calls a method from the parent class.
Abstract classes cannot be instantiated directly.
Protocol defines structural typing interfaces.
Typing module provides type annotation support.
Optional means the value can be None.
List type hints specify a list of elements.
Dict type hints specify key-value types.
Tuple type hints specify fixed-length sequences.
Any accepts any type of value.
Union allows multiple possible types.
Match statement pattern-matches values (Python 3.10+).

status = 404
match status:
    case 200:
        print('OK')
    case 404:
        print('Not found')
    case _:
        print('Unknown')

if len(lst) > 10:

    content = f.read()
# Fix:

if os.path.exists('missing.txt'):
    with open('missing.txt') as f:
        content = f.read()

example_sentence: Get_db is a common dependency for DB sessions.
code_snippet:
def get_db():
    db = SessionLocal()
    try:
        yield db
    finally:
        db.close()

db = Depends(get_db)

example_sentence: Tags organize API endpoints in documentation.
code_snippet:
router = APIRouter(prefix='/api/users', tags=['users'])

# In Swagger UI, endpoints grouped under 'users' tag

example_sentence: Response_model defines the API response structure.
Linux is the most common server operating system.
code_snippet:
# Linux commands:
ls -la
cd /home
mkdir project

example_sentence: Bash is the default Linux shell.
code_snippet:
#!/bin/bash
echo 'Hello World'
for f in *.py; do
    echo $f
done

example_sentence: SSH connects to remote servers securely.
code_snippet:
ssh user@192.168.1.100
ssh -i key.pem ec2-user@server.com

example_sentence: Chmod changes file permissions.
code_snippet:
chmod +x start.sh
chmod 755 script.py
chmod 600 secret.key

example_sentence: Systemctl manages system services.
code_snippet:
sudo systemctl start nginx
sudo systemctl enable myapp
sudo systemctl status myapp

example_sentence: Nginx is a web server and reverse proxy.
Gunicorn is a Python WSGI HTTP server.
code_snippet: gunicorn main:app --workers 4 --bind 0.0.0.0:8000

example_sentence: Uvicorn is an ASGI server for FastAPI.
code_snippet: uvicorn main:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000 --reload

example_sentence: Docker packages apps into containers.
code_snippet:
docker build -t myapp .
docker run -p 8000:8000 myapp
docker ps

example_sentence: Docker-compose manages multi-container apps.
code_snippet:
# docker-compose.yml
version: '3'
services:
  web:
    build: .
    ports:
      - '8000:8000'
  db:
    image: postgres

example_sentence: Environment variables configure apps without code changes.
code_snippet:
export DATABASE_URL='sqlite:///app.db'
export SECRET_KEY='my-secret'

import os
DB = os.getenv('DATABASE_URL')

example_sentence: Supervisor keeps apps running continuously.
code_snippet:
# /etc/supervisor/conf.d/myapp.conf
[program:myapp]
command=/usr/bin/python main.py
directory=/app
autostart=true

example_sentence: Cron runs scheduled tasks automatically.
code_snippet:
# crontab -e
# Run backup every day at 2am
0 2 * * * /usr/bin/python /app/backup.py

example_sentence: Logs record application events for debugging.
code_snippet:
import logging
logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO)
logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)
logger.info('Server started')

example_sentence: A model learns patterns from data.
code_snippet:
from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression
model = LinearRegression()
model.fit(X_train, y_train)

example_sentence: Train teaches the model using data.
code_snippet:
model.fit(X_train, y_train)
# model is now trained

example_sentence: Predict uses the model to make predictions.
code_snippet:
predictions = model.predict(X_test)
print(predictions[:5])

example_sentence: A dataset is a collection of training data.
code_snippet:
from sklearn.datasets import load_iris
data = load_iris()
X, y = data.data, data.target

example_sentence: Features are input variables for the model.
code_snippet:
X = df[['age', 'income', 'education']]
y = df['purchased']

example_sentence: Labels are the target values to predict.
code_snippet:
X = df.drop('target', axis=1)  # features
y = df['target']  # labels

example_sentence: Accuracy measures correct predictions ratio.
code_snippet:
from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score
acc = accuracy_score(y_test, predictions)
print(f'Accuracy: {acc:.2f}')

example_sentence: Loss measures how wrong the model's predictions are.
code_snippet:
import torch.nn as nn
criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()
loss = criterion(outputs, labels)

example_sentence: An epoch is one pass through the entire dataset.
Gradient indicates the direction to minimize loss.
code_snippet:
optimizer.zero_grad()
loss.backward()  # compute gradients
optimizer.step()  # update weights

example_sentence: A neural network is a model inspired by the brain.
Transformer is the architecture behind modern LLMs.
code_snippet:
from transformers import pipeline
classifier = pipeline('sentiment-analysis')
result = classifier('I love this!')

example_sentence: Tokens are the basic units of text processing.
code_snippet:
from transformers import AutoTokenizer
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained('bert-base-uncased')
tokens = tokenizer('Hello world')

example_sentence: Embeddings represent words as dense vectors.
code_snippet:
import torch.nn as nn
embedding = nn.Embedding(num_embeddings=10000, embedding_dim=256)
word_vectors = embedding(word_ids)

example_sentence: Fine-tuning adapts a pre-trained model to specific tasks.
code_snippet:
from transformers import Trainer
trainer = Trainer(
    model=model,
    train_dataset=train_data,
    eval_dataset=val_data
)
trainer.train()

example_sentence: Inference uses a trained model to make predictions.
code_snippet:
model.eval()
with torch.no_grad():
    output = model(input_data)
    prediction = output.argmax(dim=1)

example_sentence: A batch is a group of samples processed together.
code_snippet:
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader
dataloader = DataLoader(dataset, batch_size=32, shuffle=True)
for batch in dataloader:
    process(batch)
